How to use this map−
What this shows. This is a research toolkit for land and environmental defenders — the public databases, records systems and watchdog organisations you can use to investigate the corporations, developers, agencies and money behind the road, mine, pipeline, dam, logging or development threatening a place you’re trying to protect. It groups those tools by what you’re trying to find out: who really owns a company, where the public money goes, a facility’s environmental and legal record, how to pry loose documents, and who owns a parcel of land. It plots them for every jurisdiction where they exist — national, state, county and municipal. To use it, start by clicking your country and then a lit region; open any single entry to read exactly what it can — and can’t — tell you, and lean on the lens pills to the right, or the searchable index below, to narrow the whole map down to the one kind of record a particular fight needs.

Navigate. Click a country to zoom in — its regions load and any that hold resources light up. Click a lit region to open its list of research tools, and to drill deeper into its sub-divisions (state → county → municipal), since many records — property, courts, permits — are held locally. Click empty space or zoom out to step back up. Facility dots become clickable once you zoom in (about zoom 8); while zoomed out, a click selects the region beneath them.

● police, town halls, fire, gov offices, agency HQs — local buildings relevant to different campaigns (records, policing, permits, land use). Zoom in to click a dot for details (toggle off, top-right, to select regions freely).

Two ways to filter:
1· Lens pills (right) — each lens is a kind of research: corporate ownership, contracts & money, court records, environmental compliance, FOIA, property, people & networks, monitoring, watchdogs. Selecting one filters the map to that theme so you can see where those resources exist. Below the pills you can narrow further.
2· This index (below) — a directory of the resources on the map. Group & sort it (by country, level, lens…) and search by name.

How to read an entry. Every resource spells out what you can and can’t get from it, and ties it to a concrete aim — stopping a development, fighting a polluter, exposing a landlord, following political money. Sources are official government databases or independent watchdogs and newsrooms; every link is live and verified, and gaps are held honestly, never guessed.

🔥 The live projects layer — what it is
The green dots are real building projects, harvested automatically every day from official public records. The idea: almost every large project must be written down — a permit, an environmental review, a licence — before the bulldozers arrive. Those records are public. This layer collects them so a fight can start early, when it’s still winnable.

Where the data comes from. Every dot is a record from a public source, and every source is listed in the “Filter by data source” box, which you can switch on and off:
• Building permits — the US nationwide feed (all 50 states), Chicago & Seattle full history, city and regional portals worldwide, and national open-data portals.
• US government reviews — federal environmental reviews and public-land actions (mines, logging, drilling leases) from the Bureau of Land Management and Forest Service.
• Other countries — France (Sitadel), UK (PlanIt), Australia (EPBC referrals), Canada (Impact Assessment Registry), Brazil (IBAMA licences).
• Global — World Bank and development-bank construction across 138 countries, and sites physically under construction mapped by OpenStreetMap volunteers.

How to read a dot. A solid dot is a real, exact location from the record itself. A dashed ring means the source published no coordinates, so the project is shown at its country, state or town level and the exact site is somewhere in that area — open the linked record to find it. Size and colour indicate rough scale, not importance. Click any dot for the details and a link to the original record.

What it does not show. This is not every project on Earth, and it can’t be — most countries don’t publish permits at all. Coverage is deepest in the US, UK, France, Canada and Australia, thinner across Latin America, Africa and Asia, and effectively absent in Russia, China and much of the Middle East. Germany, Spain, Italy and Japan have no national register, so they appear only where individual cities publish data. The US nationwide permit feed shows the last 30 days only. Aid and development entries are filtered to physical construction — roads, dams, power, pipelines, mines — so grants, loans and training programmes are deliberately left out. It also skips small and private work: the OpenStreetMap layer keeps only sites above a size floor — very roughly a hectare of footprint for a building, about 800 m for a road or rail line — counts a quarry only where it is actually tagged as under construction rather than a long-established pit, and the city permit feeds drop householder and interior jobs, so a garage extension or a kitchen remodel never appears — only projects big enough to reshape the land.

Honesty rules. A permit is not proof a project is harmful, approved, or going ahead — it’s a public record that something is proposed. Records with impossible coordinates are re-placed at national level and flagged, never quietly deleted or guessed at.
🌐 Global Wire
The front lines — a standing overview, with a live wire beneath that tracks the developments, the companies behind them, and the land-defense fights hitting environmentally significant places.
The modern development machine runs on paper: an environmental review filed, a wetland permit sought, a rezoning buried in a legal ad no one reads. That paperwork is also its weakness — every notice is a comment window, every review a place to intervene, every permit a decision that can be challenged.

Against that machine grew a web of defenders — riverkeepers and land trusts, tribal nations and neighborhood coalitions, wilderness alliances and environmental-law firms that sue for free. They learned that a listed species, a floodplain, a buried document or a company’s own record can stop a project cold.

The fights are everywhere at once — lithium mines and data centers, pipelines and warehouses, LNG terminals and clear-cuts — and they move fast. This wire watches for them: the projects entering the pipeline, the companies behind them, and the places worth defending. Find the fight.

Map key▾
International body
Cross-border and international bodies relevant to land & environmental defence — development banks, environmental and human-rights tribunals, and watchdog networks that operate across nations. Click any gold marker to open it, just like a country.
Coverage
Has source(s) in this lens
This place has at least one verified accountability source in the lens you’re viewing — an official record-keeper or an independent watchdog.
No verified source
No verified official or watchdog source is confirmed here for this lens — a real gap, held open honestly rather than filled with a guess.
Subcategories
On the map
Live projects blends two kinds of feed. Most markers — led by OpenStreetMap’s under-construction layer, plus the Australia, Canada and World-Bank project registers — show what is being built or assessed right now, whenever it was permitted. The rest are recent permit / notice feeds, each looking back a set window (roughly 45–365 days: US Federal Register 120d · US public-land 180d · UK 180d · France 12 mo). So this is a rolling picture of what is newly permitted plus currently underway — much fuller than a one-month snapshot, but still a sample, not a census: places with no open-data feed, and projects permitted before a feed’s window that aren’t also mapped as under construction, won’t appear. The feeds don’t record completion dates, so the map can’t measure how long a project lasts — but for context, the bigger the project the longer it runs — and almost none finish within the map’s shortest window. US Census figures: a single-family home averages ~8 months start-to-completion (the large majority finish within a year); apartment & commercial buildings average ~17–20 months (mostly over a year); and large infrastructure & extraction (highways, transit, dams, power, mines) typically run 2–5+ years. So the larger projects — the ones most worth resisting — are overwhelmingly multi-year, which is why the current-state layers carry most of the picture.
Project scale — select anyAllMedium ≥$50M · 100 ac · 100 MWLarge ≥$250M · 500 acLargest ≥$1B · 2,000 ac · 500 MW
French & Indian permit entries are sized by building floor area, not land footprint — a project crossing a threshold is at least that substantial, whatever acreage it sits on.
Not size-ranked (shown at map scale by type): Australia (EPBC), Canada (IAAC), Brazil (IBAMA), and U.S. federal reviews (Federal Register, public-land NEPA) — these publish no numeric size (~12,400 sites, ~8% of all mapped).
Permit recency — dated feedsAll ages≤30d≤90d≤6mo≤1yr
